label: Interview Prep
heroTitle: Practice a simple repeatable system design approach instead of guessing under pressure.
heroDescription: These topics help you answer clearly in interviews: understand the problem, estimate scale, propose a clean design, and explain trade-offs in simple language.
cardIntro: Open any topic to see what to say, what to ask, and how to avoid common interview mistakes.

items.title: Clarify Requirements
items.summary: Before drawing boxes, make sure you understand what the product must do and what quality targets matter most.
quickPoints: Ask what the product should do first.
Then ask about scale, speed, reliability, and data rules.
Good clarification saves time and avoids wrong designs.
items.whatItMeans: Clarifying requirements means asking smart questions before designing. You want to understand features, constraints, and priorities.
items.whyItMatters: A good design for one product can be a bad design for another. Requirements change everything.
items.simpleExample: Designing chat for two people is different from designing chat for millions of group conversations with media and search.
realWorldExamples: A URL shortener may need custom aliases and analytics, or it may only need simple redirects.
A video platform may need on-demand video only, or also live streaming and comments.
A file storage service may need version history, sharing, and offline sync.
items.interviewTip: Ask just enough questions to shape the design. Do not spend the whole interview on edge cases too early.
tradeOffs: Asking too little leads to wrong assumptions.
Asking too much wastes interview time.
You should narrow scope but still show awareness of bigger concerns.
commonMistakes: Jumping to technology names before understanding the product.
Missing non-functional needs like latency or reliability.
Not stating assumptions clearly when the interviewer does not specify details.
whatInterviewerExpects: A short focused question set, not endless questions.
Good separation between functional and non-functional needs.
Clear assumptions when the problem statement is incomplete.
followUpQuestions: What would you ask first in a marketplace design interview?
How do you narrow scope when the product has many features?
Which requirement changes the architecture the most?

sampleAnswer.intro: A simple way to begin is to say you want to confirm the core use case before drawing the system.
bullets: First I would clarify the main user actions and the most important feature.
Then I would ask about scale, latency expectations, and how much stale data is acceptable.
After that I would narrow the scope so I can design one strong version instead of ten weak versions.
sampleAnswer.closing: This gives you a clear target before you choose databases, queues, or caches.

sections.title: Questions worth asking
paragraphs: Good questions usually fall into four groups: features, users, traffic, and quality expectations.
bullets: What are the main user actions?
How many users and requests do we expect?
How fresh must the data be?
What failures are acceptable or unacceptable?

items.title: Estimate Scale
items.summary: Quick estimates help you choose the right architecture instead of guessing blindly.
quickPoints: Estimate users, requests per second, data size, and storage growth.
Round numbers are fine if your reasoning is clear.
The goal is direction, not perfect math.
items.whatItMeans: Estimate scale means doing rough calculations for traffic, bandwidth, storage, and growth.
items.whyItMatters: You cannot decide whether to shard, cache, or queue work without a rough idea of system size.
items.simpleExample: If 1 million users upload 2 photos per day and each photo is 2 MB, the daily storage growth is about 4 TB before compression or deduplication.
realWorldExamples: A live chat system needs estimates for active connections, not just total users.
A video site needs bandwidth and storage estimates, not only request counts.
A feed system needs write volume and fan-out size estimates.
items.interviewTip: Say your assumptions out loud. Interviewers care more about your reasoning than exact numbers.
tradeOffs: Too much math can consume the interview.
Too little math leads to vague architecture choices.
Simple estimates are enough if they help justify design decisions.
commonMistakes: Using numbers without explaining where they came from.
Estimating total users but ignoring peak traffic.
whatInterviewerExpects: Numbers that guide design choices instead of existing in isolation.
followUpQuestions: How do peak and average traffic change the design?
What if the average object size doubles?
Which number would make you add sharding?

estimationWalkthrough.title: Example rough estimation
assumptions: Each active user sends 5 requests per minute
calculations: Peak request rate: about 16,000 to 17,000 requests per second
Peak outbound bandwidth: roughly 800 MB per second before caching
estimationWalkthrough.takeaway: These numbers are not perfect. They simply tell you that caching, replicas, and bandwidth planning are now important.

sampleAnswer.intro: You do not need perfect math. You need useful math.
bullets: I would estimate daily users, peak active users, and requests per second first.
Then I would estimate storage growth and bandwidth because those often drive architecture choices.
Finally I would use those numbers to justify caching, queues, replicas, or sharding.
sampleAnswer.closing: Simple estimates are enough if they clearly shape the design.

sections.title: Useful estimate buckets
paragraphs: You usually need only a few buckets: read QPS, write QPS, object size, daily growth, and peak multiplier.
bullets: Average traffic.
Peak traffic.
Storage per day.
Network bandwidth.

items.title: High-level Design
items.summary: After clarifying the problem, draw a simple end-to-end flow before diving into details.
quickPoints: Start with the main user flow.
Place the major components before optimizing.
Show how data moves through the system.
items.whatItMeans: High-level design is the first clean architecture sketch that shows clients, services, storage, and important supporting systems.
items.whyItMatters: A clear first draft keeps the conversation organized and gives you a base for later improvements.
items.simpleExample: For a file upload system, you might start with client -> API -> object storage -> metadata database -> async workers.
realWorldExamples: A ride app may begin with app client, gateway, matching service, trip service, and location store.
A video app may begin with upload API, storage, queue, transcoding workers, and CDN.
A news feed may begin with posting service, follow graph, feed generation service, and cache.
items.interviewTip: Do not try to show every edge case in the first diagram. Start simple, then refine.
tradeOffs: Too much detail early makes the explanation messy.
Too little detail makes it hard to discuss trade-offs.
A clear baseline lets you add depth step by step.
commonMistakes: Jumping straight into database type debates without a full flow.
Drawing too many boxes with no explanation of data movement.
Forgetting background jobs or failure handling.

stepFlow.title: Draw the client and entry point
stepFlow.description: Show where requests come from and what service receives them first.

stepFlow.title: Add the core service logic
stepFlow.description: Place the main services that make the product work.

stepFlow.title: Add storage and async work
stepFlow.description: Show where data is stored and which tasks move to queues or workers.

stepFlow.title: Finish with scaling and reliability notes
stepFlow.description: Only after the core flow is clear should you add replicas, caching, and failure handling.

whatInterviewerExpects: A clear explanation of data movement.
A layered answer that gets deeper over time.

sampleAnswer.intro: A strong high-level design answer usually sounds calm and layered.
bullets: I would start with the simplest end-to-end flow for the main user action.
Then I would add the main storage and any background processing path.
After that I would discuss where caching, replication, or partitioning may be needed.
sampleAnswer.closing: The goal is to create a clear base that can be refined, not to draw everything at once.

sections.title: A simple order to follow
paragraphs: One easy pattern is: client, entry point, core service, main storage, async processing, caching, and monitoring.
bullets: Draw the main path first.
Add scale helpers second.
Add failure and monitoring notes last.

items.title: APIs and Data Design
items.summary: A strong interview answer includes what data you store and how clients interact with it.
quickPoints: Define the main entities and key reads and writes.
Keep APIs simple and close to product actions.
Data access patterns often guide the database choice.
items.whatItMeans: This topic covers the main API endpoints, the main data objects, and how those choices affect storage and scaling.
items.whyItMatters: A system is easier to design once you know what needs to be saved, updated, and queried often.
items.simpleExample: In a URL shortener, one main write is create short link and one main read is resolve short code to original URL.
realWorldExamples: A chat app stores users, conversations, messages, and delivery states.
A ride app stores drivers, rider requests, trips, and location updates.
A file sync app stores file metadata, chunk pointers, versions, and sharing rules.
items.interviewTip: Explain the access pattern first, then justify SQL, NoSQL, search index, or object storage.
tradeOffs: Rich normalized models improve correctness, but may slow some reads.
Denormalized models speed reads, but increase write complexity.
Simple APIs are easier to reason about, but may require more backend coordination.
commonMistakes: Naming a database type without explaining access patterns.
Ignoring update frequency and query shape.
whatInterviewerExpects: Important read and write APIs are named simply.
Storage choice is tied to access pattern, not brand names.
followUpQuestions: Which APIs are read-heavy and which are write-heavy?
Would you separate metadata and blob storage?

sampleAnswer.intro: This part of the interview should sound practical, not abstract.
bullets: I would define the main entities first, such as users, posts, messages, or trips.
Then I would define the main read and write APIs around those actions.
Finally I would choose storage based on query shape, update pattern, and consistency needs.
sampleAnswer.closing: When APIs and data shape are clear, the rest of the architecture becomes easier to justify.

sections.title: How to keep it simple
paragraphs: You usually need only a few core entities and a few important APIs. Stay focused on the main user journey.
bullets: What gets created?
What gets read most often?
What changes over time?
What must be indexed?

items.title: Bottlenecks and Trade-offs
items.summary: The strongest interview answers end by showing what could break first and how you would improve it.
quickPoints: Point out the likely bottlenecks yourself.
Explain the trade-offs of your design choices.
Offer realistic next steps instead of perfect magic solutions.
items.whatItMeans: This is the part where you evaluate your own design. You identify where load, delay, cost, or failure is most likely to hurt first.
items.whyItMatters: Interviewers want to see engineering judgment, not only diagrams.
items.simpleExample: If a chat system stores all messages in one database, you can say that write scale may become a bottleneck and sharding by conversation may be a next step.
realWorldExamples: A video platform may hit storage and transcoding cost bottlenecks.
A ride platform may hit location ingestion and matching bottlenecks during rush hour.
A feed system may hit fan-out bottlenecks for very large accounts.
items.interviewTip: Volunteer the weaknesses in your own design. That shows maturity and control.
tradeOffs: More caching improves speed, but risks stale reads.
More replicas improve availability, but can increase lag.
More microservices improve separation, but increase operational complexity.
commonMistakes: Presenting the design as if it has no weaknesses.
Adding every advanced tool at once instead of growing step by step.
whatInterviewerExpects: Clear prioritization of what to fix first.
Awareness that every improvement creates a new trade-off.
followUpQuestions: Which optimization would you add first and why?
What new complexity comes after that optimization?

sampleAnswer.intro: A strong ending sounds like a thoughtful review of your own design.
bullets: The first likely bottleneck here is the write path because traffic concentrates on one storage layer.
My next improvement would be to add caching or partitioning depending on whether reads or writes are under more pressure.
That change would help scale, but it would also add complexity around consistency, invalidation, or operations.
sampleAnswer.closing: This shows that you know both how to scale and how to reason about the cost of scaling.

sections.title: A good ending structure
paragraphs: One simple way to finish is: identify the first bottleneck, propose the next improvement, then mention what new complexity that improvement adds.
bullets: What breaks first?
What would you improve next?
What new trade-off appears after that?
